Summary: This study developed a combined model incorporating radiomics and CT imaging features to differentiate between malignant and benign subcentimeter solid pulmonary nodules (SSPNs). The model demonstrated excellent discriminative ability and clinical application value, potentially aiding radiologists in diagnosing SSPNs.

Item1.reason: The study does not explicitly mention adherence to any specific radiomics or machine learning checklists or guidelines like the CLEAR checklist.

Item2.reason: The study clearly defines inclusion and exclusion criteria for patient selection, aiming to create a representative study population. The criteria include nodule size, confirmation of malignancy or benignity through pathology or follow-up, and image quality.

Item3.reason: The reference standard is well-defined, with malignant nodules confirmed by pathology and benign nodules confirmed by follow-up or pathology. The follow-up period of more than 2 years for benign nodules is appropriate. The median interval time between the CT scans and surgery was 16 days, which is appropriate.

Item4.reason: The study is a single-center retrospective study, as the data was collected from one hospital.

Item5.reason: The imaging data source is contrast-enhanced CT, which is a standard imaging technique for pulmonary nodules. The study mentions the use of standard algorithms for image reconstruction.

Item6.reason: The study reports the CT acquisition parameters, including tube voltage, tube current, helical pitch, rotation time, slice thickness, and reconstruction thicknesses. It also specifies the contrast agent and injection parameters.

Item7.reason: The median interval time between the CT scans and surgery was 16 days, which is appropriate to validate the presence or absence of target conditions of the radiomics analysis at the moment of the diagnostic imaging exams.

Condition1.reason: The study includes manual delineation of the regions of interest (ROI) of the nodules on CT images, which constitutes segmentation.

Condition2.reason: The segmentation was performed manually by a radiologist, so it is not fully automated.

Item8.reason: The study describes the segmentation methodology, specifying that a blinded radiologist manually delineated the ROIs slice by slice, avoiding adjacent vessels, bronchi, and normal lung tissue.

Item9.reason: The segmentation was performed manually, not fully automated, so this item is not applicable.

Item10.reason: The segmentation in this study was performed by a single radiologist, which reflects clinical practice.

Condition3.reason: The study extracts hand-crafted radiomic features, including first-order features, shape features, and texture features (GLCM, GLSZM, GLRLM, GLDM, and NGTDM).

Item11.reason: The study mentions normalization and resampling during image pre-processing with a fixed bin width of 25, and all images were resampled to [1,1,0] after the B-spline interpolation sampling technology. This indicates appropriate use of image preprocessing techniques with transparent description.

Item12.reason: The study used the Deepwise Multimodal Research Platform version 2.0.1.4, which is an integrated machine learning platform for medical data analysis based on the mature python Pyradiomics (version 3.0.1) and Scikit-learn (version 0.22) packages.

Item13.reason: The study describes the feature types (first-order, shape, texture) and mentions that all features were named based on the three-level naming method. The specific calculation formula for each feature can be found in the Pyradiomics web page.

Condition4.reason: The study includes tabular data in the form of radiomics features and clinical/CT characteristics organized in tables.

Condition5.reason: The study does not use an end-to-end deep learning approach. Instead, it uses hand-crafted radiomic features and clinical/CT characteristics in a logistic regression model.

Item14.reason: The study assessed the interobserver reproducibility of the segmentation using intraclass correlation coefficients (ICCs) and included features with values > 0.75 in subsequent analysis. This indicates the removal of non-robust features.

Item15.reason: The study used the analysis of variance F-test to select features, which is a method for dimensionality reduction by selecting more informative features.

Item16.reason: The study included 324 patients with 2107 radiomics features extracted. After feature selection, four radiomics features and six CT features were used to construct the combined model. The number of instances and features seems appropriate for the modeling algorithm.

Item17.reason: The study does not use an end-to-end deep learning pipeline, so this item is not applicable.

Item18.reason: The study randomly divided the dataset into training and testing cohorts in a 7:3 ratio. The model was developed using fivefold cross validation. This indicates a proper data partitioning process.

Item19.reason: The study does not explicitly discuss the handling of confounding factors. While clinical data was collected, there is no mention of analyzing or removing potential confounders.

Item20.reason: The study reports appropriate accuracy metrics, such as AUC, accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value, and negative predictive value. ROC curves were also drawn to compare the diagnostic accuracy of the models.

Item21.reason: The study reports 95% confidence intervals (CI) for the AUC values, which is a measure of uncertainty.

Item22.reason: The study does not mention any calibration assessment of the final model.

Item23.reason: The study does not perform uni-parametric evaluations to justify the need for a multi-parametric approach. It uses contrast-enhanced CT images and combines them with clinical features without comparing to a uni-parametric approach.

Item24.reason: The study compares the performance of the combined model with clinical and radiomics models. The DCA was used to calculate the clinical impact of the models.

Item25.reason: The study does not compare the performance of the models with a simple baseline reference model (such as a Zero Rules/No Information Rate classifier).

Item26.reason: The model is tested on an independent data set (testing cohort) that is sampled from the same source as the training cohort.

Item27.reason: The study does not include external testing with independent data from other institutions.

Item28.reason: The study states that the datasets are not publicly available due to data sharing not obtaining the informed consent of the patients.

Item29.reason: The study does not mention sharing any scripts related to automatic segmentation and/or modeling with the public.

Item30.reason: The study does not mention sharing the final model in any form with the public.
